<!-- PROSE — edit freely in Obsidian -->

Three linked sketches drawn from Tolstoy's own experience as an artillery officer during the Siege of Sevastopol (1854–55) in the Crimean War. Written in an unflinching documentary style — the second sketch in the present tense, directly addressing the reader — they stripped away the romantic glamour of war and depicted death, fear, and boredom with unsparing honesty. The sketches made Tolstoy famous in Russia almost overnight and established the anti-heroic realism that would define his later fiction.
